items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNP?
items.correct: GDP measures output produced within a country's borders; GNP measures output produced by a country's nationals wherever they are.
items.misconception: GDP and GNP are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: GDP is income and GNP is spending.
items.why: Foreign profits from a UK firm abroad count in UK GNP but not UK GDP. Profits of a Japanese firm in the UK count in UK GDP but Japan's GNP. The distinction matters for globalised economies.

items.q: What is the difference between real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI?
items.correct: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP adjusts for inflation; GNI (Gross National Income) is GDP plus net income from abroad (similar to old GNP). Each answers a different question about economic activity.
items.misconception: Real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI are interchangeable terms.
items.random_wrong: Real GDP is GDP only from physical goods; nominal GDP includes services.
items.why: Each measure has a precise role. Comparing growth over time needs real GDP; comparing across years uses nominal cautiously; GNI captures returns from foreign holdings. Students treat them as synonyms and misuse data.

items.q: Is a trade deficit necessarily bad?
items.correct: Not necessarily — it can reflect strong domestic demand, attractive investment opportunities, or borrowing to fund growth. Context matters more than the headline figure.
items.misconception: A trade deficit always means a country is losing wealth and failing economically.
items.random_wrong: A trade deficit means a country has no factories.
items.why: The US has run deficits for decades while growing. Trade deficits are balanced by capital inflows. Students treat deficits as automatic failure, missing the accounting identity and what it implies.
It's 1995. “1945” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Viner. “Ricardo” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the WTO established?
items.why: The real figure is 1995 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
